item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the closest source ([https://www.dailysquib.co.uk/most-popular/2276-lady-gaga-to-present-amputated-leg-to-lucky-fan.html here) shows no indication it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't shake the feeling there is something more to this article:

1. We cannot look into the containment chamber for obvious reasons, we can only hear what is going on.
2. The D-class that enter the chamber stop communicating entirely with the research staff and also do not seem to communicate anything odd upon exiting.
3. Despite being provided with surgical tools the operation is almost always performed haphazardly with non surgical equipment. Equipment that is materialising in the containment chamber through unknown means.

Are we 100% sure the D-class are doing this to themselves?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
apotemnophilia anyone?
This SCP idea makes me unnerved and intrigued. I love this SCP concept even more because of this!
+1

item.title: How is this Euclid?
item.content:encoded: If it decays at a normal rate then how has it not self neutralized yet, especially if testing has been prohibited?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Four words:
What the actual fuck

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

In SCP-1311 it is mentioned that the SCP-1311 survivors are immune to the compulsion effects of SCP-1993. Such an ability would almost certainly be worthy of a footnote in the SCP-1993 documentation in-universe. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

All humans making visual contact with SCP-1993 will become immediately convinced that SCP-1993 is their leg, amputated from them at some point in the past and replaced with a simulacrum. Affected individuals will have no explanation as to how, when, or under what circumstances their leg was removed, but in no instance has any individual been disabused of the idea once exposed to SCP-1993. If permitted physical access to SCP-1993, individuals will find some manner of removing their own leg above the knee; documented incidents have seen individuals applying tourniquets and performing amputations with improvised sharpened pieces of metal, shards of glass, or their own teeth, cutting through skin, muscle, and tendon, dislocating the patella and severing all requisite tendons and the anterior cruciate ligament.

To this:

All humans making visual contact with SCP-1993 will become immediately convinced that SCP-1993 is their leg, amputated from them at some point in the past and replaced with a simulacrum1. Affected individuals will have no explanation as to how, when, or under what circumstances their leg was removed, but in no instance has any individual been disabused of the idea once exposed to SCP-1993. If permitted physical access to SCP-1993, individuals will find some manner of removing their own leg above the knee; documented incidents have seen individuals applying tourniquets and performing amputations with improvised sharpened pieces of metal, shards of glass, or their own teeth, cutting through skin, muscle, and tendon, dislocating the patella and severing all requisite tendons and the anterior cruciate ligament.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. Survivors of SCP-1311 are immune to this effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually think that a recovery log would be great here, for one simple reason: they would have to have used an MTF of all-blind agents. Which the foundation presumably keeps around for really specific cases like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The basic premise is fucking brilliant enough, but the materializing of mystery fixing agents? Genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a question: how would this affect someone who was born without either their right or both of their legs?

item.title: Typo?
item.content:encoded: Not sure if this is a mistake or what, but the "winner" of the last test's gimme match (D-8923)'s new leg originally belonged to one D-51684, who is not mentioned at any other point on the page. I think it may be intended to be D-1864, who is one of the Ds taking part in that experiment, but I'm not completely sure. So, can this get fixed/clarified or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm disappointed in the lack of testing with subjects already missing that leg. (but still +1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the second paragraph of the description, it says

If permitted physical access to SCP-1993, individuals will find some manner of removing their own leg below the knee;

Surely, this is supposed to be above the knee, to match the mode of amputation stated in the first paragraph

SCP-1993 is a human right leg, severed at an angle beginning above the knee and descending to the patella.

Maybe I'm misunderstanding the anatomical terminology here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who couldn't stop thinking of that recurring gag in SpongeBob where the guy keeps yelling "MY LEG!"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I… actually, I didn't like this much. As soon as I read the containment procedures, I thought 'this is going to be the opposite of phantom limb, isn't it?' and indeed it was, and it didn't surprise me at all at any point. It just seemed to be piles of squick, which doesn't appeal to me too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why… why is my leg up there?
Honestly this one is amazing in my opinion. Very creepy.
+1
Unsettling. Take your hundredth up vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because it somehow obtained a staple gun and a roll of duct tape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted, however, why is it Euclid? It's not trying to get out of it's box…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually a slight twist on a very real thing called Asomatognosia, a deficiency that makes you unable to recognize which parts of your body belong to you. Oliver Sacks describes it a bit in his second book, talking about a man who believed a sawed-off corpse's leg was put into his bed and somehow attached itself to him. I don't think that resemblance was intended by the author of this one, but for some reason it makes this SCP a little creepier to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One more question: will the thing work on actual amputees?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In short:
1. A detailed recovery log would probably be far more complicated than helpful, as it would just be "and then X saw the leg and took it and put it on, and then Y saw the leg and took it and put it on, (repeat repeat repeat) and then the last host is in a containment chamber." Probably a few instances of armed guards getting into gunfights over the leg. It's basically just that over and over again, and I didn't figure it would be helpful. The answer to the second question being "a bunch."
2. What Anaxagoras said. This thing is certainly mundane, but not inert. It's not doing anything terribly outlandish, but what it is doing, it's doing in some indiscernible way.
3. I imagine something like blinders, or the containment chamber (since there's one dedicated chamber for this SCP) has an automated cleaning apparatus. Possibly robotic instruments could move the device? I hadn't thought about it, to be honest. Actually, you could maybe have test subjects at least replace their original leg (the new SCP-1993) into the safe, since they're crazy, but still not insubordinate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd say the testing log shows why it's not safe. They have no idea what this thing's properties are, beyond the fake leg thing. The manifestation of the staple gun and duct tape suggest some sort of reality warping or something very strange.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. I would really like to know how this object was found and contained. And how many agents lost their legs in the process.
  2. Why is this not safe? When put in a box this leg happily decomposes over the course of a month.
  3. I would also like to know how are they cleaning up after and how does the leg get back into its safe.

All in all, the idea is good but the execution is flawed somewhat. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCPs name is now "Absence of Duct Tape".

That is all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So they have all these surgery tools and they go like this.
"Screw this, I'll use duct tape."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eeeeeeeeeeeeewwww hahaha I like it. I personally didn't mind the duct tape or staple guns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"30 seconds of a squirting sound" YEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEWWWWWWWWWW

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded. The rest of this is just wonderfully macabre and unsettling. Spontaneous generation of tools for attachment just seems a little extraneous and unnecessary, distracting from to the very well constructed visceral gutpunch of the rest of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just wanted to second this opinion. Article is fine in all other respects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. Not sure what I think about the magical appearance of duct tape etc, but I am intrigued by it, so not quite a downside. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful, but honestly I'd prefer it without the magically appearing items or compass rose tattoo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A while back, I had to go for surgery to correct the patella on my right leg. I distinctively remember my brother asking me if the doctor had amputated my leg while I was still high on anesthetics.

This brings back bad memories. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Idea reminds me of… another post 1000 SCP, I can't recall which. There are a lot of SCPs that make you think you own them.

But this one gets an upvote as well, because of its excellent execution.

… also.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i was thinking that the apparating duct tape, etc was a bit off-topic, but then I caught another detail. All the severed legs have the same compass tatoo. So this effect had poorly understood, more than mental, physcial world effects from the get-go.

Can previous subjects get re-effected? Because that could be a whole different level of squick in the group test. Subject "A" wins the fist fight, and does the self-surgery, which transfers the effect to A's former leg. B is the first of the others to regain consciousness, so he immediately attaches As former leg. A sees the newly anomalous former B leg, and immediately starts ripping out staples to repeat the attachment. Wash, rinse, and repeat. I think, if everyone survived long enough, you would eventually get people who were correct. That actually is their leg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird as hell, but there's something going on here that I like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How did you sick fucks get a picture of my leg?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but end up giving up and just using staple guns and duct tape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the opposite of alien hand syndrome. Instead of the person thinking the part isn't theirs, they think a detached leg belongs to them. I wonder if other parts are out there somewhere? Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right, yes. That's correct. My read on it was that they were attempting microsurgery even when it was obviously impossible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only if the vascular tissues were reconnected, I believe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the body dysmorphic leg!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't know what injuries could be caused by trying to walk on a poorly-attached leg in this manner besides ripping open scabbing that's occurred to the stump. Unless you meant fall injuries, in which case I'd specify.

"Falling or stumbling" was what immediately popped into mind, and it really amused me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me laugh, especially the "MINE" and "GIMMEE" and the sudden materialization of duct tape

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rhabdomyolisis is another potential cause of death if a necrotic limb were sewn onto the body.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this except for the mysterious appearance of items to attach with. That just seems like a real departure from the premise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a huge fan of the idea, but well-written as always.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
or injuries caused by attempting to walk on the attached leg.

Not sure what's meant by this. Don't know what injuries could be caused by trying to walk on a poorly-attached leg in this manner besides ripping open scabbing that's occurred to the stump. Unless you meant fall injuries, in which case I'd specify.

In the event that the patient survives the procedure's immediate aftermath, infections resulting either from general exposure to bacteria or from contact with necrotic tissue will typically prove lethal without immediate medical treatment.

So, exposure to bacteria, or exposure to bacteria, then? The way this sounds, it would probably be sepsis. Could simply say this, remove what's ultimately a redundant statement, and don't need to state the obvious - that massive systemic blood-borne bacterial infection kills you without antibiotics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is fantastic. Well done Eskobar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah Eskobar what the fuck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gong to need to work on the picture somewhat when I get back later. But I was imagining that this person began at the mid thigh and cut at an angle toward the kneecap. I'll work on it too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you would have to be some sort of Yoga-instructor/Werewolf hybrid to chew your own leg off.

Also, the picture doesn't match the description - it appears to be cleanly severed through the femur.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: looks good to me! I like this one, but the sudden appearance of the staple gun and duct-tape in the containment seems a bit tacked in there, and probably should be expanded on or revised.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture is blurry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I literally don't know what was going on in my head when I wrote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is weird, but good.
